headlines.geopolitics: Global Food Supply Faces a Dangerous Bottleneck as Iran War Persists
headlines.economy: Versant, Owner of MS NOW, in Talks to Acquire Vox Media's Podcast Division
headlines.domestic: 'Ship has sailed': This is what Dems won't get in DHS deal after shunning GOP

clips.caption: Duke explains why his wife Jenny controls their grocery budget
Jax's dating app disaster gets uncomfortably specific
Pringle discovers what a thirst trap is

script.text: I told her we should sell and she said 'and live where, Deepak?' She only calls me Deepak when I've said something stupid.
Good morning. I'm Leo Barros, and this is The Friction for Thursday, March twenty-seventh.

The war in the Middle East is creating a global food crisis most Americans haven't noticed yet. Fertilizer prices are climbing twenty-three percent this quarter as supply chains through Iran and the Persian Gulf face continued disruption.

Iran produces roughly twelve percent of the world's phosphate fertilizers. Russia and Belarus, also under sanctions, control another thirty percent of global potash exports. Agricultural economists are warning that spring planting in both the U.S. and Europe could see reduced yields if these disruptions continue through April.

The Biden administration is weighing whether to ease sanctions on Russian fertilizer exports, a move that would undercut broader efforts to isolate Moscow but could prevent food shortages in developing nations. State Department officials tell us no decision has been made, but the clock is ticking on planting season.
In media consolidation news, Versant Corporation, parent company of CNBC and MS NOW, is in advanced talks to acquire Vox Media's entire podcast division for an estimated four hundred million dollars.

The deal would give Versant control of major shows including The Ezra Klein Show and Today Explained, as traditional cable companies continue pivoting toward digital audio. Vox would retain its written journalism and video operations. Industry analysts see this as part of a broader flight from podcast independence as production costs rise and advertising revenues plateau.

On Capitol Hill, the second-longest Department of Homeland Security shutdown in U.S. history ended early Friday morning, but Democrats walked away empty-handed after two months of political brinksmanship.

Senate Democrats had demanded sweeping reforms to ICE and Border Patrol operations following fatal shootings in Minnesota. They wanted judicial warrants for immigration arrests, requirements for agents to display visible identification, and restrictions on enforcement near schools and hospitals.

Instead, they got twenty million dollars for body cameras, which was already in the original funding bill. Senate Majority Leader John Thune said Democrats 'kissed that opportunity goodbye' by blocking funding attempts. Republicans retained full immigration enforcement funding and plan to extend it for up to a decade using budget reconciliation.
The political miscalculation leaves Democrats with little leverage as the administration prepares to ramp up deportation operations this spring.
Professor, this fertilizer crisis feels unprecedented, but you've seen supply chain shocks disrupt global politics before.

Indeed. October nineteen seventy-three. The Arab oil embargo. King Faisal of Saudi Arabia and President Sadat of Egypt coordinated a petroleum embargo against nations supporting Israel in the Yom Kippur War.

Within six months, oil prices quadrupled from three dollars to twelve dollars per barrel. But the real damage wasn't just at the gas pump. The embargo exposed how completely the global economy depended on a handful of chokepoints controlled by hostile actors.
How did that reshape American foreign policy?

It forced Nixon and Kissinger to completely recalibrate Middle East strategy. They realized that America's industrial might meant nothing if a few Arab kings could shut down our economy with a phone call. The Strategic Petroleum Reserve, created in nineteen seventy-five, was a direct response.

But here's the crucial difference. Oil has substitutes, eventually. You can drill elsewhere, develop alternative energy, change consumption patterns. Food production has no substitutes. People must eat, and they must eat every day.
So this fertilizer shortage could be more disruptive than the oil shocks?

Potentially, yes. The nineteen seventy-three embargo lasted five months. Food shortages topple governments. The French Revolution began with bread riots. The Arab Spring started when a Tunisian fruit vendor couldn't afford to eat.
If Iran can hold global agriculture hostage, they've discovered a weapon more powerful than nuclear missiles.
That's your Download for Thursday. Now let's talk about what all of this actually means for real people. Jax, Duke, you've been listening.
Okay, so we're all gonna starve because some fertilizer got caught up in a war? That's the most twenty twenty-six headline ever.

Look, I went to the grocery store last weekend and Jenny handed me a list. I'm scanning barcodes with my phone to check prices before I put anything in the cart. Dude, I felt like I was conducting an orchestra of anxiety.
You're checking prices on your phone while shopping?
Jenny installed this app called Basket. It tracks grocery inflation in real time. She's got charts, Leo. Charts of our cereal expenses going back eighteen months.
Your wife tracks your cereal expenses?
She tracks everything. Last month she showed me a spreadsheet proving we spend more on the kids' goldfish food than I spend on lunch. The goldfish, Jax. They're eating better than I am.
I find it remarkable that we've digitized the ancient practice of household budgeting to such a granular degree. Peggy kept our expenses in a ledger book for forty years.

Professor, Jenny's not keeping a ledger. She's conducting economic warfare against inflation. I suggested we switch to generic cereal and she pulled up a cost-per-serving analysis that would make the Congressional Budget Office weep.
This is why I'm single. I can't handle that level of financial accountability. Like, if someone tracked my DoorDash spending, they'd stage an intervention.
Speaking of interventions, Mia called me out on my grocery spending last week. She's fourteen and apparently more financially literate than I am.
What happened?
I'm picking her up from her mom's house and I mention I need to stop at Whole Foods. Mia looks at me and says, 'Dad, why do you shop at Whole Paycheck when you complain about money all the time?'
script.direction: Imitating his daughter's voice, slightly mortified

script.text: Did she actually call it Whole Paycheck?
She did. Then she pulls up the Stop & Shop app on her phone and shows me I could save thirty-seven dollars on the exact same grocery list. My fourteen-year-old daughter is now my financial advisor.
Did you take her advice?
Of course I did. I'm not an idiot. But now she texts me grocery store coupons. Coupons, from my teenager. I've never felt older in my life.
Okay, but can we talk about the real issue here? Leo, are you actually complaining about money to your daughter? Because that's like, therapy-level concerning.
I don't complain to her. I complain near her. There's a difference. She just has very good hearing and an alarming ability to retain financial information.
Look, the twins asked me last week why I yelled at the gas pump. Apparently I said something about the price of premium that they found educational.
What did you say?
I may have referred to the oil companies using language that Jenny described as 'colorful' and the twins described as 'new vocabulary words for school.'
I'm curious about this grocery price-tracking phenomenon. Is this a generational response to economic uncertainty, or have we simply made anxiety more efficient through technology?

Professor, we've made everything more anxious through technology. I got a notification yesterday that my Uber rating dropped to four-point-eight-seven and I spent twenty minutes wondering what I did wrong in a car ride I don't remember taking.
Four-point-eight-seven is still good though, right?
Duke, I don't know! That's the problem! Is four-point-eight-seven good for a passenger? What's the curve? Am I failing at sitting in cars?
How do you fail at being a passenger?
I don't know! Maybe I smell weird? Maybe I breathe too loud? Maybe I'm not making enough small talk? Or maybe I'm making too much small talk? There's no manual for this stuff!
You're overthinking it. Just sit there and don't be weird.
script.direction: Practical, trying to calm him down

script.text: But what if weird is my default setting? What if I've been weird this whole time and nobody told me?
Jax, we've told you. Many times.
That's not helping!
Can we circle back to the media consolidation story? Because I have thoughts about Versant buying up podcast companies.
script.direction: Trying to steer back to news

script.text: You mean like how they're gonna turn every podcast into cable news?

Exactly. These cable companies are panicking because nobody under forty watches cable. So they're buying up digital properties and trying to recreate the same advertising model that's already failing.
Wait, so they're gonna put ads in the middle of podcasts? Like, actual TV commercial breaks?
They're already doing it. Have you listened to a Spotify podcast lately? It's three minutes of content, two minutes of ads, repeat until everyone's miserable.

This reminds me of the newspaper consolidation of the nineteen eighties. Local ownership disappeared, editorial independence vanished, and eventually the whole industry collapsed under its own financial engineering.
So podcasts are gonna die too?
Not die. Just become terrible. There's a difference.
Great. So in five years, every podcast will be three dudes in suits reading marketing copy about mattresses.
You say that like it's not already happening.
Which brings me to my next confession. I tried to start a dating podcast last month.
Oh no.
I thought, you know, I'm single, I have opinions about dating apps, maybe I can monetize my romantic failures. Turns out that's not actually a business model.
What happened?

I recorded three episodes. The first one was called 'Why Hinge is Hell.' The second one was 'Bumble: An Anthropological Study.' The third one was just me reading Tinder profiles out loud for forty-seven minutes.
Please tell me you didn't publish the Tinder one.
I published all three. Got seventeen downloads total. Sixteen of them were from my mom, who left a one-star review saying I need therapy.
Your mom left a one-star review on your own podcast?
She said, and I quote, 'My son clearly has unresolved issues with intimacy and should not be giving dating advice to anyone, especially strangers on the internet.' End quote.
script.direction: Reading it like a formal statement

script.text: Your mother reviewed your podcast?
She has a Google account and strong opinions, Professor. It's a dangerous combination.
Who was the seventeenth download?
A woman named Sarah who super-liked me on Tinder, then found my podcast, listened to the episode where I read her profile out loud, and unmatched me. She also left a review.
What did she say?
Quote: 'This man violated my privacy and has the emotional intelligence of a houseplant. Also, he mispronounced my last name.' Unquote.
You mispronounced her name while reading her dating profile on a podcast?
script.direction: Amazed at the layers of failure

script.text: Look, Kowalczyk is not an easy name, okay? I did my best with limited information!
I'm trying to understand the logic of this enterprise. You attempted to attract romantic partners by publicly mocking other romantic prospects?
When you put it like that, it sounds bad.
It sounds bad because it is bad, Jax.
Fine! I deleted the podcast. Happy? But now I'm back to being single with no monetization strategy.
Have you considered that being single might not require a monetization strategy?
Leo, everything requires a monetization strategy. That's capitalism.
That's not capitalism. That's just you being weird about money.
I find it fascinating that your generation treats personal relationships as economic opportunities. Peggy and I met at a library book sale. There was no monetization involved.
Professor, with respect, book sales don't scale. I need solutions that work for the digital economy.
You need solutions that work for actual human beings, not the digital economy.
What is a 'thirst trap,' and why does my granddaughter keep asking if I've posted one?
Oh no. Oh no no no.
Professor, you don't want to know.
I most certainly do want to know. She manages my social media accounts and yesterday she said something about my Twitter photo being 'surprisingly thirsty.' I have no idea what that means.
What's your Twitter photo?
It's a photograph of me in my garden, holding a prize-winning tomato. I'm wearing my reading glasses and a Virginia Cavaliers hat. Very dignified, I thought.
And your granddaughter called that thirsty?
She said, and I'm quoting directly, 'Grandpa, you look like a silver fox professor daddy in this photo. The comments are unhinged.' I don't understand any of those words in that order.
Jesus Christ.
Professor, I think your granddaughter is saying that middle-aged women find you attractive on the internet.
Because of my tomato?
Not because of the tomato, Professor.
Then what? I'm seventy-three years old. I teach economic history. I own cardigans.
script.direction: Listing reasons why this makes no sense

script.text: Professor, some people are into that. There's a whole thing about distinguished older men with, uh, intellectual authority.
Intellectual authority is attractive?
You've been married. How is this news to you?
Peggy married me despite my intellectual authority, not because of it. She said I was too serious and needed someone to make me laugh.
Right, but now you're accidentally posting thirst traps on Twitter and your granddaughter has to manage your accidental sex appeal. That's hilarious.
I am not posting— what did you call them? Thirst traps. I am posting photographs of my garden.
Professor, intent doesn't matter on the internet. If people think your tomato photo is hot, your tomato photo is hot.
That's the most ridiculous thing I've ever heard.
And on that note, let's move to some lighter news.
Okay, quick round of weird news. A Secret Service agent shot himself in the leg yesterday while protecting Jill Biden at an airport.
How do you shoot yourself in the leg while on protection duty?
The report says 'accidental discharge during routine security procedures.' Which sounds like government speak for 'nobody wants to explain this.'
Is he okay?
He's fine. Jill Biden reportedly asked if he needed a band-aid. In other Florida news, a man was arrested for lying on top of his car during a hailstorm instead of filing an insurance claim.
He used his body as hail protection?
For three hours. Neighbors called police because they thought he was having a mental health crisis. Turns out he just really loves that car and doesn't trust insurance companies.
I mean, I get it. Insurance companies are the worst.
Also, Nepal's new Prime Minister delivered his inaugural address as a rap song, and another Florida man was arrested for shooting alligators from an airboat while drunk.
The Prime Minister of Nepal rapped his inauguration speech?
script.direction: Focused on the most surprising element

script.text: Apparently it's going viral on TikTok. The chorus translates to something like 'I will serve the people with beats and policy.' It's actually pretty catchy.
Political rhetoric has certainly evolved since the Gettysburg Address.
That's The Friction for Thursday. I'm Leo Barros.
Duke Patel. Jenny says I'm not allowed to complain about grocery prices in front of the kids anymore.
Dr. Haywood Pringle. I shall be researching what constitutes a thirst trap and whether I should be concerned about my tomatoes.
And I'm Jax Mooney, still accepting applications for a girlfriend who won't review my life choices on Google. See you tomorrow.
